title: Emergency evaluation of spontaneous pneumothorax
content: Spontaneous pneumothorax commonly presents with acute dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain. Chest radiography remains a first-line imaging test, and urgent intervention is warranted if tension physiology is suspected. Clinical deterioration, hypoxemia, or hemodynamic instability should prompt immediate escalation.

title: Secondary pneumothorax in chronic lung disease
content: Secondary pneumothorax often occurs in patients with underlying chronic lung disease and may produce more severe hypoxemia than primary spontaneous pneumothorax. Management decisions depend on symptom burden, size of pneumothorax, and physiologic reserve.

journal: American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine
title: Radiographic pitfalls in acute dyspnea
content: Pulmonary embolism can coexist with or mimic thoracic emergencies that present with acute dyspnea. A discordance between imaging findings and bedside physiology should trigger reconsideration of the differential diagnosis and further testing.

title: Community-acquired pneumonia imaging update
content: Lobar consolidation on chest radiography, particularly when accompanied by fever and cough, strongly supports community-acquired pneumonia. Small pleural effusions may accompany infection and can indicate a parapneumonic process.

title: Pleural disease and bedside imaging
content: Pleural effusion and pneumothorax are both well suited to rapid bedside assessment with ultrasound. When chest radiography is equivocal, ultrasound may improve speed and confidence of diagnosis.

title: Pulmonary edema on acute chest imaging
content: Diffuse or basilar hazy opacities with dyspnea should raise concern for pulmonary edema, particularly in the presence of cardiovascular risk factors or volume overload. Bedside ultrasound and natriuretic peptide testing can help differentiate edema from infection.
